questions.prompt: What should be in scope for this goal?
questions.description: The repo already separates client use from Worker-level Temporal runtime features. This goal can stay focused on the client transport or expand into broader runtime/protobuf work.
questions.recommendedAnswer: Implement Cloudflare Workers support for @temporalio/client by adding a gRPC-Web/fetch transport path, documenting how to use it, and adding automated checks that prevent Node gRPC from entering the Worker-safe path. Exclude running Temporal Workers in Cloudflare Workers and exclude a full protobuf runtime migration unless a small transport-required protobuf change is unavoidable.

questions.prompt: How should users opt into the gRPC-Web transport?
questions.description: The current connection module imports Node gRPC at top level, so a Worker-compatible API likely needs a path that bundlers can load without Node-only dependencies.
questions.recommendedAnswer: Provide an explicit Worker-safe entry point or factory for gRPC-Web, such as a Cloudflare/fetch connection entry point, rather than relying only on runtime environment detection.

questions.prompt: What endpoint should the gRPC-Web transport target?
questions.description: Cloudflare Workers can issue fetch requests, but gRPC-Web usually requires either a server that directly supports gRPC-Web or a proxy translating gRPC-Web to native gRPC.
questions.recommendedAnswer: Assume the SDK talks to a gRPC-Web compatible endpoint and clearly document that users may need a proxy in front of Temporal Server or Temporal Cloud if the target endpoint does not directly support gRPC-Web.

questions.prompt: Which existing client behaviors must work through gRPC-Web in the first pass?
questions.description: This helps define what success means beyond making one RPC work.
questions.recommendedAnswer: Support the client APIs that use unary service RPCs, preserve static metadata and API key headers, preserve deadlines/timeouts and abort cancellation where fetch supports them, and map gRPC-Web failures into the SDK's existing service error shape closely enough for existing retry/error helpers to keep working.

questions.prompt: What verification should the final plan require?
questions.description: Automated checks can prove TypeScript behavior and bundling safety; a real Cloudflare deployment test may require credentials or extra infrastructure.
questions.recommendedAnswer: Require unit tests for request encoding, response decoding, metadata, deadlines/aborts, and error mapping; require a Worker-bundle smoke test that imports the Worker-safe entry point without Node gRPC dependencies; document a manual end-to-end check against a gRPC-Web compatible Temporal endpoint if CI cannot provide one.
